Section 1.title: Conversation 1
Section 1.Definition 1.1: For a squarefree Q where 6 | Q, let UQ be the set of residues r (mod Q) such that both r and r + 2 are coprime to Q. The residue-pairing bound is: BQ(x) = ∑ r∈UQ min (π(x;Q, r), π(x;Q, r + 2)) where π(x;Q, a) is the count of primes p ≤ x with p ≡ a (mod Q).
Section 1.Conjecture 1.1: For all x ≥ 7 and any squarefree Q with 6 | Q: π2(x) ≪ BQ(x)
Section 1.Conjecture 1.3: (Monotone refinement). If Q1 | Q2 are both squarefree and divisible by 6, then for every x ≥ 7: BQ2(x) ≤ BQ1(x). This implies that as we refine the modular partition, the sum of modular minima is non-increasing.
Section 1.Definition 1.2: Writing aQ(r;x) := π(x;Q, r) for r ∈ UQ, one has the exact identity: BQ(x) = 1/2 ∑ r∈UQ (aQ(r;x) + aQ(r + 2;x) − |aQ(r;x) − aQ(r + 2;x)|).
Section 1.Conjecture 1.4: Another computationally sharp inequality is: π2(x) ≪ 1/2 SQ(x) − 1/2 VQ(x) where SQ(x) := ∑ r∈UQ (aQ(r;x)+aQ(r+2;x)) is the total mass in admissible classes and VQ(x) := ∑ r∈UQ |aQ(r;x) − aQ(r + 2;x)| is the summed class-discrepancy.
Section 1.Conjecture 1.5: A coarser π-only corollary replaces 1/2(aQ(r;x)+aQ(r+2;x)) by min(aQ(r;x), aQ(r+2;x)) ≤ 1/2(aQ(r;x) + aQ(r + 2;x)), eliminating explicit absolute values: π2(x) ≤ 2 + 1/2 (π(x) − ∑ a∈FQ π(x;Q, a) − cQ) where FQ := {a (mod Q) : gcd(a,Q) = 1 but gcd(a + 2, Q) ≠ 1} are forbidden start-classes and cQ removes the small primes dividing Q (explicit below per modulus).
Section 1.Definition 1.3: For Q1 | Q2, let the fiber FQ2→Q1(r1) := {r2 ∈ UQ2 : r2 ≡ r1 (mod Q1)}.
Section 1.Conjecture 1.6: If Q1 | Q2 are squarefree with 6 | Q1, then for all x ≥ 7: BQ2(x) ≤ ∑ r1∈UQ1 min (∑ r2∈F aQ2(r2;x), ∑ r2∈F aQ2(r2 + 2;x)) ≤ BQ1(x).
Section 1.Conjecture 1.7: (Exact fiber cancellation identity (Gap decomposition)). For each r1 ∈ UQ1, let L(r1;x) and R(r1;x) be the sums of the left and right counts in the fiber. The fiber’s cancellation deficit is: δr1(x) := 1/2 [ ∑ r2∈F |dr2(x)| − | ∑ r2∈F dr2(x) | ] ≥ 0 where dr2(x) = aQ2(r2;x) − aQ2(r2 + 2;x). The total gap splits as: BQ1(x) − BQ2(x) = ΦQ1←Q2(x) + ∑ r1∈UQ1 δr1(x) where Φ is the loss from residue classes that become forbidden modulo Q2.
Section 1.Conjecture 1.8: (Strict Drop). Fix squarefree Q and prime p ≥ 5 coprime to Q. There exists X0 such that for all x ≥ X0: BpQ(x) ≤ BQ(x) − 1 (SD).
Section 1.Definition 1.4: (general h-step tuples). We define: • Let h ∈ 2N and m ≥ 1. An h-step (m+ 1)-tuple is a set of primes {p, p+ h, . . . , p+mh}. • For a modulus Q ≥ 2 define the admissible starts U(h,m)Q := {r (mod Q) : gcd(r + jh,Q) = 1 for all j = 0, . . . ,m}. • For a residue-class prime count aQ(a;x) := π(x;Q, a) and a tuple-minimum over a fixed start r: M(h,m)Q(r;x) := min 0≤j≤m aQ(r + jh;x). • Define the modular minima bound: B(h,m)Q(x) := ∑ r∈U(h,m)Q M(h,m)Q(r;x).
Section 1.Conjecture 1.9: For all x ≥ 7: Nh,m(x) ≤ Ch,m(Q) + B(h,m)Q(x).
Section 1.Conjecture 1.10: (Example). Cousin primes (h=4), m=1 Q=6: admissible start r ≡ 1 (mod 6); end r + 4 ≡ 5 (mod 6). For x ≥ 7: π4(x) ≤ 1 + min{π(x; 6, 1), π(x; 6, 5)}. The constant 1 accounts for the exceptional pair (3, 7).

Section 2.title: Conversation 2
Section 2.Definition 2.1: Let π2(x) := #{p ≤ x−2 : p and p+2 are prime}. π4(x) := #{p ≤ x−4 : p and p+4 are prime}. π(x; q, a) is the count of primes ≤ x that are ≡ a (mod q).
Section 2.Conjecture 2.1: For all x ≥ 7: π2(x) + π4(x) ≤ π(x−4) + 2.
Section 2.Conjecture 2.2: There exist absolute constants c1, c2 > 0 and x0 such that for all x ≥ x0: c1 · (π(x;3,2) − π(x;3,1))/log x − x/(log x)3 ≤ π2(x) − π4(x) ≤ c2 · (π(x;3,2) − π(x;3,1))/log x + x/(log x)3.
Section 2.Definition 2.2: For even h ≥ 2, write πh(x) := #{p ≤ x−h : p and p+h are prime}. For modulus q ≥ 1 and residue a modulo q, let π(x; q, a) := #{p ≤ x : p prime, p ≡ a (mod q)} and ∆q(a;x) := π(x; q, a) − π(x)/ϕ(q). Define the admissibility mask Kq,h(a) := 1gcd(a(a+h),q)=1.
Section 2.Conjecture 2.3: Let r ≥ 3 be an odd prime. Then for all x ≥ 2r: ∑ j=1..r−1 π2j(x) ≤ (r − 2) · π(x − 2(r − 1)) + Or(1).
Section 2.Conjecture 2.4: Universal mod-q pair-bias inequality relating πh1(x) − πh2(x) to Rq;h1,h2(x) with error ≤ C(q,h1,h2)·x/(log x)3.
Section 2.Conjecture 2.5: Concrete specialization: For q multiple of 6, Rq;2,4(x) = (1/log x)(π(x;6,5) − π(x;6,1)).
Section 2.Conjecture 2.6: Exact Triad Identities linking B6,2(x), B6,4(x), B6,0(x) and Chebyshev bias terms.
Section 2.Conjecture 2.7: Elementary Start/End Class Caps and Aggregate Obstruction inequalities.
Section 2.Conjecture 2.8: Bias inequality: |π2(x) − π4(x) − (π(x;6,5) − π(x;6,1))/log x| ≤ C · x/(log x)3.
Section 2.Definition 2.4: Defines bias transform Bq,h(a;x), min-bias aggregator Fq;2,4(x), and small prime counts ωq(x).
Section 2.Conjecture 2.10: Simple universal bounds and equality cases for Fq;2,4(x).
Section 2.Definition 2.5: Definitions of ∆q(a;x), masks Kq,2 and Kq,4, bias Bq,h(x), and prime divisor counts ωq(x).
Section 2.Conjecture 2.11: Relations between Sq(x), Dq(x), Bq,2(x), Bq,4(x).
Section 2.Conjecture 2.12: Ellipse law describing geometric relation between Bq,2 and Bq,4.
Section 2.Conjecture 2.13: Product identity 4Bq,2(x)Bq,4(x) = Sq(x)² − Dq(x)² and associated bounds.
Section 2.Conjecture 2.16: Sign constraints and bracketing inequalities for Bq,2(x) and Bq,4(x).

Section 3.title: Conversation 3
Section 3.Definition 3.1: Definitions of πh(x), residue r(h), Sh(x), weighted sets H2 and H4.
Section 3.Conjecture 3.2: Start-Normalized Mod-6 Parity Inequality bounding weighted normalized gap differences by C/log x.
Section 3.Conjecture 3.3: Special cases including twin vs cousin and symmetric gap families.
Section 3.Definition 3.2: Definitions for πh(x;q,a), fibers F5(q), F1(q), and aggregated counts Π2(x;q), Π4(x;q).
Section 3.Definition 3.3: Definitions for tuples ∆, fibers Fr(∆;q), aggregated counts Π∆(x;q,r).
Section 3.Conjecture 3.6: Intra-family Fiber Balance for Sexy Pairs and Chains.
Section 3.Conjecture 3.7: Cross-family Coupling between complementary fiber orientations.

Section 4.title: Conversation 4
Section 4.Definition 4.1: Defines Π2(x), Π4(x), π(x;3,a), ∆pairs(x,y), ∆3(x,y), twin-prime constant C2, and λ(x).
Section 4.Conjecture 4.1: Twin-Cousin Linear Response in Short Intervals with bound |∆pairs(x,y) − λ(x)·∆3(x,y)| ≤ K·√y/(log x)2.
Section 4.Conjecture 4.2: Cumulative form: |Π2(t) − Π4(t) − (2C2/log t)(π(t;3,2) − π(t;3,1))| ≤ K·√t/(log t)2.

Section 5.Definition 5.1: Defines π2(x), π4(x), semiprime count Q(x), and discrepancy ∆h(x).
Section 5.Definition 5.2: Interval definitions for π2(I), π4(I), residues Pr(I) modulo 12, and ratio R(I).
Section 5.Conjecture 5.3: Residue-ratio sandwich for twin vs cousin pairs.
Section 5.Conjecture 5.4: Robust form with universal distortion κ.
Section 5.Definition 5.3: Modulus 30 setup with residue classes and start/target sets for gaps 2,4,6.
